Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - MVP-BERT: REDESIGNING VOCABULARIES FOR CHI - NESE BERT AND MULTI VOCAB PRETRAINING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Despite the development of pre-trained language models (PLMs) significantly raise the performances of various Chinese natural language processing (NLP) tasks, the vocabulary for these Chinese PLMs remain to be the one provided by Google Chinese Bert Devlin et al. (2018), which is based on Chinese characters. Second, the masked language model pre-training is based on a single vocabulary, which limits its downstream task performances. In this work, we first propose a novel method, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , to form the vocabulary of Chinese BERT, with the help of Chinese word segmentation (CWS) and subword tokenization. Then we propose three versions of multi-vocabulary pretraining (MVP) to improve the models expressiveness. Experiments show that: (a) compared with char based vocabulary, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ does not only improves the performances of Chinese PLMs on sentence level tasks, it can also improve efficiency; (b) MVP improves PLMs’ downstream performance, especially it can improve _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ’s performances on sequence labeling tasks.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The pretrained language models (PLMs) including BERT Devlin et al. (2018) and its variants Yang et al. (2019); Liu et al. (2019b) have been proven beneficial for many natural language processing (NLP) tasks, such as text classification, question answering Rajpurkar et al. (2018) and natural language inference (NLI) Bowman et al. (2015), on English, Chinese and many other languages. Despite they brings amazing improvements for Chinese NLP tasks, most of the Chinese PLMs still use the vocabulary (vocab) provided by Google Chinese Bert Devlin et al. (2018). Google Chinese Bert is a character (char) based model, since it splits the Chinese characters with blank spaces. In the pre-BERT era, a part of the literature on Chinese natural language processing (NLP) first do Chinese word segmentation (CWS) to divide text into sequences of words, and use a word based vocab in NLP models Xu et al. (2015); Zou et al. (2013). There are a lot of arguments on which vocab a Chinese NLP model should adopt.

The advantages of char based models are clear. First, Char based vocab is smaller, thus reducing the model size. Second, it does not rely on CWS, thus avoiding word segmentation error, which can directly result in performance gain in span based tasks such as named entity recognition (NER). Third, char-based models are less vulnerable to data sparsity or the presence of out-of-vocab (OOV) words, and thus less prone to over-fitting (Li et al. (2019)). However, word based model has its advantages. First, it will result in shorter sequences than char based counterparties, thus are faster. Second, words are less ambiguous, thus may be helpful for models to learn the semantic meanings of words. Third, with a word based model, exposure biases may be reduced in text generation tasks (Zhao et al. (2013)). Another branch of literature try to strike a balance between the two by combining word based embedding with character based embedding Yin et al. (2016); Dong et al. (2016).

In this article, we try to strike a balance between the char based model and word based model and provides alternative approaches for building a vocab for Chinese PLMs. In this article, there are three approaches to build a vocab for Chinese PLMs: (1) following Devlin et al. (2018), separate the Chinese characters with white spaces, and then learn a sub-word tokenizer (denote as _char_ ); (2) first segment the sentences with a CWS toolkit like jieba[1] , and then learn a sub-word tokenizer (denoted

as _seg tok_ ); (3) do CWS and kept the high-frequency words as tokens and low-frequency words will be tokenized by _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ (denoted as _seg_ ). See Figure 1 for their workflow of processing an input sentence. Note that the first one is essentially the same with the vocab of Google Chinese BERT.

Inspired by the previous work that incorporate multiple vocabularies (vocabs) or combine multiple vocabs in an natural wayYin et al. (2016); Dong et al. (2016), we also investigate a series of strategies, which we will call Multi-Vocab Pretraining (MVP) strategies. The first version of MVP is to incorporate a hierarchical structure to combine the char based vocab and word based vocab. From the viewpoint of model forward pass, the embeddings of Chinese characters are aggregated to form the vector representations of multi-gram words or tokens, which then are fed into transformer encoders, and then the word based vocab will be used in masked language model (MLM) training. We will denote this version of MVP as MVP _hier_ . Note that in MVP _hier_ , the char based vocab is built by splitting the Chinese words in the word based vocab into Chinese chars, and non-Chinese tokens are kept the same. We will denote this strategy as MVP _hier_ ( _V_ ), where _V_ is a word based vocab.

The second version of MVP (denoted as MVP _pair_ ) is to employ a pair of vocabs in MLM. Due to limited resources, in this article we only consider the pair between _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ and _char_ . MVP _pair_ is depicted in Figure 2(c). In MVP _pair_ , a sentence (or a concatenation of multiple sentences in pretraining), is processed and tokenized both in _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ and _char_ , and the two sentences are encoded by two parameter-sharing transformer encoders. Whole word masking Cui et al. (2019b) is applied for pretraining. For example, the word ” `篮球` ” (basketball) is masked. The left encoder, which is with _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , has to predict the single masked token is ” `篮球` ”, and the right encoder has to predict ” `篮` ” and ” `球` ” for two masked tokens. MLM loss from both sides will be added with weights. With MVP _pair_ , parameter sharing enables the single vocab model to absorb information from the other vocab, thus enhancing its expressiveness. Note that after pre-training, one can either keep one of the encoder or both encoders for downstream finetuning. We will denote this strategy as MVP _pair_ ( _V_ 1 _, V_ 2 _, i_ ), where _V_ 1 and _V_ 2 are two different vocabs, _i_ = _s_ means only the encoder with _V_ 1 is kept for finetuning (single vocab model), and _i_ = _e_ means both encoders are kept (ensemble model).

The third version of MVP (denoted as MVP _obj_ ) is depicted in Figure 2(b). In MVP _obj_ , the sentence is encoded only once with a fine-grained vocab, and MLM task with that vocab is conducted. As in the figure, he word ” `喜欢` ” (like) is masked, and under the vocab of _char_ , the PLM has to predict ” `喜` ” and ” `欢` ” for the two masked tokens. As additional training objective, we will employ a more coarse-grained vocab like _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ and ask the model to use the starting token (” `喜` ”)’s representation to predict the original word under _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ . We will denote this strategy as MVP _pair_ ( _V_ 1 _, V_ 2), where _V_ 1 and _V_ 2 are a pair of vocabs and _V_ 1 is the more fine-grained one.

pretraining, the three vocab building methods and three MVP strategies are compared on a series of Chinese benchmark datasets, 4 of which are sentence classification tasks, 3 are sequence labeling tasks. The experimental results demonstrate the following take-aways: 1) directly use Chinese words as vocab ( _seg_ ) does not perform well; 2) _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ based ALBERT consistently performs better than _char_ and _seg_ on sentence classification tasks, but not sequence labeling tasks; 3) MVP strategies can help to improve a single vocab model on both types of tasks, especially it can help _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ on sequence labeling tasks; 4) MVP _pair_ ensemble are the best model, but it comes with a higher inference time; 5) MVP _obj_ help to provide a better single vocab model than MVP _pair_ .

We now summaries the following contributions in this work.

- experiments on three ways of building new vocab for Chinese BERT, and _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , the combination of CWS and subword tokenization is novel.

- We propose 3 MVP strategies for enhancing the Chinese PLMs.

## 2 RELATED WORK

Before and since Devlin et al. (2018), a large amount of literature on pretrained language model appear and push the NLP community forward with a speed that has never been witnessed before. Peters et al. (2018) is one of the earliest PLMs that learns contextualized representations of words. GPTs Radford et al. (2018; 2019) and BERT Devlin et al. (2018) take advantages of Transformer Vaswani et al. (2017). GPTs are uni-directional and make prediction on the input text in an auto-regressive manner, and BERT is bi-directional and make prediction on the whole or part of the input text. In its core, what makes BERT so powerful are the pretraing tasks, i.e., Mask language modeling (MLM) and next sentence prediction (NSP), where the former is more important. Since

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

BERT, a series of improvements have been proposed. The first branch of literature improves the model architecture of BERT. ALBERT Lan et al. (2019) makes BERT more light-weighted by embedding factorization and progressive cross layer parameter sharing. Zaheer et al. (2020) improve BERT’s performance on longer sequences by employing sparser attention.

The second branch of literature improve the training of BERT. Liu et al. (2019b) stabilize and improve the training of BERT with larger corpus. More work have focused on new language pretraining tasks. ALBERT Lan et al. (2019) introduce sentence order prediction (SOP). StructBERT Wang et al. (2019) designs two novel pre-training tasks, word structural task and sentence structural task, for learning of better representations of tokens and sentences. ERNIE 2.0 Sun et al. (2019) proposes a series of pretraining tasks and applies continual learning to incorporate these tasks. ELECTRA Clark et al. (2020) has a GAN-style pretraining task for efciently utilizing all tokens in pretraining. Our work is closely related to this branch of literature by design a series of novel pretraining objective by incorporating multiple vocabularies. Our proposed tasks focus on intra-sentence contextual learning, and it can be easily incorporated with other sentence structural tasks like SOP.

Another branch of literature look into the role of words in pre-training. Although not mentioned in Devlin et al. (2018), the authors propose whole word masking in their open-source repository, which is effective for pretraining BERT. In SpanBERT Joshi et al. (2019), text spans are masked in pretraining and the learned model can substantially enhance the performances of span selection tasks. It is indicated that word segmentation is especially important for Chinese PLMs. Cui et al. (2019a) and Sun et al. (2019) both show that masking tokens in the units of natural Chinese words instead of single Chinese characters can significantly improve Chinese PLMs. In this work, compared to literature, we propose to re-design the vocabulary of the Chinese BERT by combining word segmentation and sub-word tokenizations.

## 3 OUR METHODS

In this section, we present our methods for rebuilding the vocab for Chinese PLMs, and introduce our series of MVP strategies.

## 3.1 BUILDING THE VOCABS

We investigate four work-flows to process the text inputs, each corresponding to a different vocab (or a group of vocabs) (Figure 1). We first introduce the single vocab models, _char_ , _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ and _seg_ . For char based vocab _char_ , Chinese characters in the corpus are treated as words in English and are separated with blank spaces and a sub-word tokenizer is learned. _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ requires the sentences in corpus to be segmented and a sub-word tokenizer like BPE are learned on the segmented sentences. Note that in _seg tok_ , some natural word will be splitted into pieces, but there are still many tokens that have multiple Chinese chars. Finally, _seg_ with size _N_ is built with the following procedures: a) do CWS on the corpus; b) count the words’ frequency, and add the tokens from _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ with frequency 0; c) for long tail Chinese words and non-Chinese tokens, tokenize them into subword tokens with _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , and the words’ frequencies are added to the sub-word tokens’ frequencies; d) sort the vocab via frequency, and if the most frequent N words or tokens can cover 99.95% of the corpus, then take them as vocab. Note that some of the tokens from _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ will be dropped.

## 3.2 MULTI-VOCAB PRETRAINING (MVP)

In this subsection, we will introduce MVP, which is a derivation of MLM task by Devlin et al. (2018). MVP has three versions, MVP _hier_ , MVP _obj_ and MVP _pair_ , all of which have one thing in common, that is, they require more than one vocab to implement pre-training.

Figure 2(a) depicts the architecture of MVP _hier_ and Figure 2(a) depicts its procedure for processing input sentences. Two vocab, a fine-grained vocab _Vf_ , and a more coarse-grained vocab _Vc_ , are combined in a hierarchical way. Sequences are first tokenized via _Vc_ , and then the Chinese tokens (if containing multiple Chinese characters) are splitted into single characters. Chinese characters and non-Chinese tokens are embedded into vectors. Then representations of chars inside a word is aggregated into the representation of this word, which is further fed into the transformer encoder. During MLM task, whole word masking is applied, that is, we will mask 15% of the tokens

In MVP _obj_ , a sentence is tokenized and embedded in a fine-grained _Vf_ (e.g., a char based vocab), and MLM task on _Vf_ is conducted. However, different from the vanilla MLM, another MLM task based on a more coarse-grained vocab _Vc_ is added. For example, encoded representations of the chars ” ~~`喜`~~ ” and ” `欢` ” inside the word ” ~~`喜`~~ `欢` ” is aggregated to the vector representation of the word, and an auxiliary MLM layer is tasked to predict the word based on _Vc_ . For the aggregator in the example, we adopt BERT-style pooler, which is to use the starting token’s representation to represent the word’s representation.[4] Denote **x** _[mask]_ and **y** _[mask]_ as the masked tokens under _Vf_ and _Vc_ respectively. Now we introduce MVP _pair_ , which is the most resource-demanding version of MVP, but it will be proven beneficial. Our goal is to enhance the model with a single vocab, by introducing additional encoder with another vocab and a corresponding MLM task. In this strategy, a sentence is tokenized and embedded with two vocabs, _V_ 1 and _V_ 2, which will be fed into separate transformer encoders. Transformer encoders will share the same parameters, but the embedding layers are separate. For example in Figure 2(c), ” ~~`喜`~~ `欢` ” is masked, and thus two tokens on the left sequence and(or) one token on the right sequence are masked. The left encoder and MLM layer is tasked to recover the two tokens ” ~~`喜`~~ ” and ” `欢` ”. And on the right, the other MLM layer needs to recover ” ~~`喜`~~ `欢` ”. Through parameter sharing, self-supervised signals from one vocab is transferred to the model with the other vocab. Formally, Thus the loss function for MVP _pair_ is

If after MVP _pair_ pretraining we decide to only keep one of the encoder, we will call this model single vocab MVP _pair_ . Otherwise, we can call the model as ensemble MVP _pair_ . For single mode MVP _pair_ , finetuning is the same with vanilla ALBERT. Finetuning for ensemble mode MVP _pair_ is different. For sentence classification, the pooled vectors on both [CLS] tokens will be concatenated to be the feature vector of the classifier. When doing sequence labeling tasks, two ways of ensemble can be conducted, which we will use the example from Figure 2(c) to illustrate. The first approach is to concatenate the features from fine-grained encoder to the coarse-grained encoder. That is, representations of ” ~~`喜`~~ ” and ” `欢` ” are aggregated to the representation of ” ~~`喜`~~ `欢` ”, and it will be concatenated to the representation from the coarse-grained encoder on the right. We will call this approach fine-to-coarse ensemble. The other approach is coarse-to-fine ensemble, which is to concatenate the representation of ” ~~`喜`~~ `欢` ” from the coarse-grained encoder to ” ~~`喜`~~ ” and ” `欢` ” from the fine-grained encoder. Then the labels of ” ~~`喜`~~ ” and ” `欢` ” are predicted.

For notational convenience, we will denote the model pretrained with MVP _hier_ strategy and vocab _V_ as MVP _hier_ ( _V_ ). MVP _obj_ with a fine-grained vocab _Vf_ and a coarse-grained vocab _Vc_ are denoted

MVP _pair_ with two vocab _V_ 1 and _V_ 2 is denoted as MVP _pair_ ( _V_ 1 _, V_ 2 _, i, j_ ), where _i_ can be _single_ , meaning only keep the encoder from _V_ 1 for finetuning, or _ensemble_ , meaning keep both encoders, and _j_ can be _ftc_ (short for fine-to-coarse) or _ctf_ (coarse-to-fine), which is two approaches for finetuning MVP _pair_ on sequence labeling tasks. Note that if _i_ equals _single_ , we will neglect the parameter _j_ .

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

## 4.1 SETUP

For pre-training corpus, we use Chinese Wikipedia. The vocab size is 21128 for _char_ and _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ . 50.38% of _seg_ are Chinese tokens with length more than 1.
In this article, we adopt jieba as our CWS tool.[5] To keep 70% of the vocab as natural words from CWS, and cover 99.95% of the corpus, _seg_ is built with vocab size 69341.

For MVP _hier_ , we consider MVP _hier_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ). For MVP _obj_ , we consider MVP _obj_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) and MVP _obj_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, seg_ ), with _λ_ equal to 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 10.0. For MVP _pair_ , we consider MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ), the combination of _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ and char based vocab, with _λ_ equal to 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 10.0. Note that to maintain the consistency for non-Chinese tokens, the char based vocab in MVP _hier_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ), MVP _obj_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) and MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) is derived from _seg tok_ by splitting Chinese characters of _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ tokens into characters, not exactly the same with _char_ .

For pretraining, whole word masking is adopted, and total 15% of the words (from CWS) in the corpus are masked, which are then tokenized into different tokens under different vocabs. For MVP _obj_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) and MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ), 1/3 of the time only tokens _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ are predicted, and 1/3 of the time only tokens from the derived char based vocab are predicted, and for the rest of the time, tokens from both vocabs are predicted.

In this article, all models use the ALBERT as encoder. We make use of a smaller parameter settings, that is, the number of layer is 3, the embedding size is 128 and the hidden size is 256. Other ALBERT configurations remain the same with ALBERT Lan et al. (2019). The pretraining hyper-parameters are almost the same with ALBERT Lan et al. (2019). The maximum sequence length is 512. Here, the sequence length is counted under the coarse-grained vocab for MVP _hier_ , fine-grained vocab for MVP _obj_ , and the longer one of the two sequences under the two vocabs for MVP _pair_ . The batch size is 1024, and all the model are trained for 12.5k steps. The pretraining optimizer is LAMB and the learning rate is 1e-4. For finetuning, the sequence length is 256, the learning rate is 2e-5, the optimizer is Adam Kingma & Ba (2015) and the batch size is set as the power of 2 and so that each epoch contains less than 1000 steps. Each model is run on a given task for 10 times and the average performance scores and standard deviations are reported for reproducibility.

## 4.2 BENCHMARK TASKS

For downstream tasks, we select 4 text classification tasks: (1) ChnSentiCorp (chn)[6] , a hotel review dataset; (2) Book review[7] (book ~~r~~ eview), collected from Douban[8] by Liu et al. (2019a). For sentence pair classification tasks, we include the following 4 datasets: (1) XNLI (xnli) from Conneau et al. (2018) ; (2) LCQMC (lcqmc) Liu et al. (2018); (3) NLPCC-DBQA[9] (nlpcc ~~d~~ bqa), a question-answer matching task in the open domain; (4) Law QA Liu et al. (2019a), a QA matching task in the legal domain. We also investigate three NER tasks. MSRA NER (msra) Levow (2006) is from open

## 4.3 EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS

We first compare the three single vocab models. We find that word based vocabs _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ and _seg_ perform better than _char_ on all sentence classification tasks, and _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ outperforms _seg_ even though having less parameters. However, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ is worse than or comparable to _char_ on three NER tasks, where these performance gaps are only partially explained by the CWS errors[12] . We believe that its disadvantages on sequence labeling tasks is due to sparcity of tokens, i.e., each token has less training samples, thus are less well trained for token level classification. From the above results, we show that both CWS and subword tokenization are important to improve the expressiveness of Chinese PLMs in sentence level tasks via vocab re-designing. In addition, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ is more efficient than _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ . we can observe a 1.35x speed up when changing the vocab from _char_ to _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ .

The lower rows of Table 1 also demonstrates the effectiveness of MVP training. Note that in this table, we report MVP _obj_ with _λ_ = 2 _._ 0, and MVP _pair_ with _λ_ = 1 _._ 0. First, we can see MVPs consistently improve the models’ performances, among which the largest improvements come from MVP _obj_ and ensemble MVP _pair_ . Second, for _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , keeping only one encoder provides improvements on sequence labeling tasks, but the improvements are less than that provided by MVP _pair_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, seg_ ) on sentence level tasks. Third, note that for NER tasks, ensemble MVP _pair_ performs better than single MVP _pair_ , and coarse-to-fine ensemble is more effective than fine-to-coarse ensemble. Fourth, MVP _obj_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, seg_ ) achieve performances that are close to MVP _pair_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, char, e_ ) on lcqmc, with less than half of the time in pre-training and fine-tuning. But the model’s improvements on NER tasks are less significant. Notice that for pretraining and finetuning, MVP _pair_ takes almost twice the resources and time than MVP _obj_ . Thus, for resources limited scenarios, MVP _obj_ could be a more suitable choice.

## 4.4 EFFECTS OF _λ_

In this section, we investigate how the relative importance coefficient _λ_ for MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) affects the ALBERT models’ downstream performances. Here for MVP _pair_ , _λ_ is assigned to the loss term from coarse-grained vocab. We set _λ_ equal to 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 10, and run per-

For ensemble MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, e_ ), _λ_ = 1 _._ 0 provides the best performances. And for MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, s_ ), increasing _λ_ worsen the performance on NER, but not on sentence level tasks, and vice versa for MVP _pair_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, char, s_ ).

## 5 CONCLUSIONS

In this work, we first propose a novel method, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , to re-build the vocabulary of Chinese BERT, with the help of Chinese word segmentation (CWS) and subword tokenization. Then we propose three versions of multi-vocabulary pretraining (MVP) to improve the models’ performance. Experiments show that: (a) compared with char based vocabulary, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ does not only improves the performances of Chinese PLMs on sentence level tasks, it can also improve efficiency; (b) MVP improves PLMs’ downstream performance, especially it can improve _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ’s performances on sequence labeling tasks.

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a critical and long-standing debate in Chinese Natural Language Processing: the trade-off between character-based and word-based vocabularies for Pre-trained Language Models (PLMs). The authors contribute a new vocabulary construction method, *seg_tok*, and a suite of Multi-Vocab Pretraining (MVP) strategies designed to capture both fine-grained and coarse-grained linguistic information. The quality of the high-level motivation is strong, as the reliance on the original Google Chinese BERT character vocabulary is indeed a bottleneck for the field. However, several significant issues detract from the paper’s clarity and formal rigor. First, the writing is hindered by inconsistent notation and typographical errors—most notably the frequent, distracting strikethroughs in "*seg_tok*" and the naming of MVP objectives (e.g., the text refers to MVP_obj but then uses the notation MVP_pair to describe it). Second, the architectural descriptions of the three MVP versions are somewhat brief; while Figure 2 provides a conceptual overview, the paper lacks formal mathematical definitions for the loss functions and the exact aggregation mechanisms in MVP_hier. Finally, the novelty of "Whole Word Masking" and "hierarchical embeddings" needs better positioning against existing work like BERT-WWM or MacBERT to clarify the specific delta of this paper's contribution. Overall, while the proposed methods are intuitive and potentially impactful for efficiency and performance, the manuscript requires a thorough proofreading and more formal technical specification to meet the standards of a top-tier conference.

comments.title: Notation Confusion between MVP_pair and MVP_obj
comments.quote: We will denote this strategy as MVP _pair_ ( _V_ 1 _, V_ 2), where _V_ 1 and _V_ 2 are a pair of vocabs and _V_ 1 is the more fine-grained one.
comments.explanation: In the description of the third version of MVP (MVP_obj), the authors define the notation as 'MVP_pair(V1, V2)'. This is a clear notation inconsistency because they have just finished defining the second version as 'MVP_pair' and are currently describing 'MVP_obj'. Using the 'pair' label for the 'obj' strategy notation would confuse readers trying to distinguish between the dual-encoder approach (pair) and the single-encoder auxiliary objective approach (obj). This appears to be a copy-paste error where 'pair' was written instead of 'obj', as confirmed by the paper summary and the experimental results section.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'seg' vocabulary construction logic
comments.quote: (3) do CWS and kept the high-frequency words as tokens and low-frequency words will be tokenized by seg t ok (denoted as seg ).
comments.explanation: The construction of the 'seg' vocabulary is described as a hybrid of CWS and the 'seg_tok' method. However, the explanation of how frequencies are handled is circular and underspecified. Specifically, it states: 'add the tokens from seg tok with frequency 0; c) for long tail Chinese words... tokenize them into subword tokens with seg tok, and the words’ frequencies are added to the sub-word tokens’ frequencies'. If high-frequency words are kept as single tokens but low-frequency words are decomposed via a subword tokenizer (seg_tok) that itself was trained on CWS-segmented text, it is unclear if there is a overlap in the vocabulary indexes or how the model handles a word that is 'high-frequency' but also happens to be a valid sub-token of a 'low-frequency' word. This ambiguity makes it difficult to reproduce the exact vocabulary mapping used in 'seg'.

comments.title: Reference Mismatch for Figure 2 sub-architectures
comments.quote: Figure 2(a) depicts the architecture of MVP hier and Figure 2(a) depicts its procedure for processing input sentences.
comments.explanation: The text states that Figure 2(a) depicts both the architecture and the procedure for MVP_hier. However, looking at the provided contexts for MVP_obj and MVP_pair, the authors use (a), (b), and (c) to distinguish between the three different strategies. The second mention of 'Figure 2(a)' in this sentence likely intended to refer to Figure 1 (which illustrates the tokenization workflow) or was a typo for a different sub-figure. This duplication creates ambiguity regarding which diagram illustrates the 'procedure' versus the 'architecture'.

comments.title: Unspecified Aggregator Selection in Loss Function
comments.quote: Denote **x** [mask] as the masked tokens under Vc . The loss function for MVP hier is [Equation missing but text says] in which I [x] i [is a variable...]
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the authors describe the hierarchical aggregation for MVP_hier (aggregating character embeddings to form word representations). While they later specify for MVP_obj that they use a 'BERT-style pooler' (starting token representation), they do not explicitly define the aggregation function (e.g., mean pooling, max pooling, or the first-token approach) used for MVP_hier. Since this aggregation is the core mechanism allowing character-based inputs to be processed by a transformer expecting word-based tokens, the lack of specification makes the architecture difficult to reproduce exactly. The note in footnote 4 suggests they consider aggregators an area for future work but fails to confirm the default for MVP_hier.

comments.title: Inconsistency in MVP Variable Definitions and Mappings
comments.quote: Denote **x** [mask] and **y** [mask] as the masked tokens under Vf and Vc respectively.
comments.explanation: The text contains multiple notation swaps and contradictory variable definitions. In the description of MVP_hier, sequence x is associated with the coarse-grained vocabulary (Vc). However, in MVP_obj, x is defined as the fine-grained vocabulary (Vf) and y as the coarse-grained (Vc). Furthermore, the text description of the MVP_obj mask indicator (I[y]_j) as indicating 'masking in sequence y' contradicts the fact that MVP_obj uses a single input stream (x). This inconsistency makes the joint loss functions and implementation logic difficult to follow.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Sequence Labeling Ensemble Strategy
comments.quote: The first approach is to concatenate the features from fine-grained encoder to the coarse-grained encoder. That is, representations of ” ~~喜~~ ” and ” 欢 ” are aggregated to the representation of ” ~~喜~~ 欢 ”, and it will be concatenated to the representation from the coarse-grained encoder on the right.
comments.explanation: For the 'fine-to-coarse' ensemble, the authors describe aggregating fine tokens to coarse representations. However, sequence labeling (NER) typically requires a prediction for every character to handle fine-grained boundaries. The authors do not clarify how they handle cases where coarse tokens do not align with NER entity boundaries, which is the primary reason character-based models are preferred in Chinese NLP. Additionally, the specific aggregation method (e.g., mean pooling) is missing, and the 's' (single) model row's performance on NER tasks is missing from Table 1 without explanation.

comments.title: Missing Loss Equation for MVP_pair
comments.quote: Formally, Thus the loss function for MVP _pair_ is
comments.explanation: The text introduces the formal definition of the loss function for the MVP_pair strategy but terminates abruptly without providing the equation. While the summary provided in the context includes a loss for MVP_hier and MVP_obj, it is missing the specific formulation for MVP_pair. Given that this strategy involves two parameter-sharing encoders and two separate MLM heads, the specific mathematical definition is necessary for understanding and reproducibility.

comments.title: Potential Contradiction in Masking Strategy for MVP_obj and MVP_pair
comments.quote: For MVP _obj_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) and MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ), 1/3 of the time only tokens _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ are predicted, and 1/3 of the time only tokens from the derived char based vocab are predicted, and for the rest of the time, tokens from both vocabs are predicted.
comments.explanation: The authors state that for MVP_obj, they sometimes only predict coarse-grained tokens (seg_tok). However, the definition of MVP_obj relies on a single fine-grained encoder. Omitting the fine-grained prediction (the primary task) for 1/3 of the training might conflict with the goal of using the encoder to learn stable representations. Furthermore, this temporal/stochastic sampling strategy (1/3 frequencies) is not reflected in the provided Loss Equations, which show a fixed joint summation across both vocabularies using a scalar lambda.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Full Wikipedia' Pre-training Duration
comments.quote: The batch size is 1024, and all the model are trained for 12.5k steps.
comments.explanation: The authors train an ALBERT model on Chinese Wikipedia for 12.5k steps with a batch size of 1024 (~12.8 million samples). For a corpus size like Wikipedia, this is an unusually short pre-training duration (typically 100k+ steps). This raises concerns about whether the models reached sufficient convergence to validly compare the architectural benefits of different vocabulary strategies, as these advantages often materialize during later stages of training.

The definition of $I[y]_j$ as masking in sequence $y$ is slightly ambiguous—it likely should simply be an indicator of which coarse tokens are being predicted based on the masks in $x$.

comments.title: Inconsistency in $MVP_{pair}$ naming convention
comments.quote: We will denote this strategy as $MVP_{pair}(V_1, V_2)$.
comments.explanation: In the 'third version of MVP' (which the text explicitly labels as $MVP_{obj}$), the authors conclude the paragraph by saying, 'We will denote this strategy as $MVP_{pair}(V_1, V_2)$'. This appears to be a typo where $MVP_{obj}$ was intended. Given that $MVP_{pair}$ is a distinct strategy described in the preceding and succeeding paragraphs with different architectural properties (dual encoders vs. single encoder), using the $pair$ subscript for the $obj$ definition is confusing for the reader. The summary also indicates $V_{obj}$ was expected here.

comments.title: Discrepancy in λ definition for $MVP_{pair}$ loss
comments.quote: $\mathcal{L}_{MVP_{pair}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{l_x} I[x]_i \log P(x_i | \mathbf{x}) - \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{l_y} I[y]_j \log P(y_j | \mathbf{y})$
comments.explanation: In the summarized equations, $\lambda$ is applied to the second vocabulary's MLM loss. However, in Section 3.2, the text states for $MVP_{pair}$: 'MLM loss from both sides will be added with weights.' While $\lambda$ effectively weights the second loss relative to the first, Section 4.1 states that for $MVP_{pair}$, 1/3 of the time only $V_1$ is predicted, 1/3 only $V_2$, and 1/3 both. This temporal/probabilistic weighting in the implementation (stochastic gradient contribution) is slightly different from a fixed scalar $\lambda$ multiplier on a joint loss as presented in the equation. A reader trying to reproduce the 1.0 optimal $\lambda$ might find the 'weights' and the '1/3 frequency' descriptions at odds.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Sequence Labeling Ensemble Logic
comments.quote: The first approach is to concatenate the features from fine-grained encoder to the coarse-grained encoder. That is, representations of ”喜” and ”欢” are aggregated to the representation of ”喜欢”, and it will be concatenated to the representation from the coarse-grained encoder on the right.
comments.explanation: The authors describe two ensemble modes: $ftc$ (fine-to-coarse) and $ctf$ (coarse-to-fine). In $ftc$, they aggregate fine tokens to the coarse representation. However, sequence labeling (like NER) typically requires character-level or fine-grained predictions to maintain precision and avoid CWS boundary errors. If they aggregate to the coarse representation, the output space of the model is reduced to $V_c$ tokens. The paper later claims $ctf$ is better for NER. The concern is that the $ftc$ method as described seems to lose the very benefit of character-level granularity that the paper argues is necessary for sequence labeling. The method of aggregation (mean pooling? max pooling?) is also not specified.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a well-motivated and timely investigation into improving Chinese BERT models through vocabulary redesign and multi-vocabulary pretraining strategies. The core idea—rethinking the default character-based vocabulary of Chinese BERT by integrating word-level information via Chinese word segmentation (CWS) and subword tokenization—is both conceptually sound and practically relevant, given the ongoing debate between character- versus word-based modeling in Chinese NLP. The introduction effectively frames this tension and positions the proposed *seg_tok* vocabulary and Multi-Vocab Pretraining (MVP) framework as a balanced solution. However, the paper suffers from several critical issues that affect clarity, rigor, and reproducibility. Most notably, the exposition is marred by inconsistent notation, unpolished prose, and fragmented presentation—such as incomplete sentences (“After pretra”), misplaced figure references, and undefined symbols (e.g., “Albert **?**”). Additionally, the methodological descriptions of MVP variants lack sufficient detail for full reproducibility, particularly regarding how representations are aligned or combined across vocabularies and how losses are weighted in practice. The absence of baseline comparisons with established non-character-based Chinese BERT variants (e.g., BERT-wwm or Chinese-BERT-base) also weakens the empirical grounding. While the proposed approaches show promise, especially in improving sequence labeling performance, the current version requires substantial revision to achieve the clarity, completeness, and technical precision expected at ICLR. The ideas are strong but currently under-served by the presentation.

comments.title: MVP_obj incorrectly labeled as MVP_pair in text description
comments.quote: We will denote this strategy as MVP _pair_ ( _V_ 1 _, V_ 2), where _V_ 1 and _V_ 2 are a pair of vocabs and _V_ 1 is the more fine-grained one.
comments.explanation: Multiple issues flag the same core problem: the authors incorrectly refer to MVP_obj using the notation 'MVP_pair' in Section 1, immediately after describing the MVP_obj strategy. This mislabeling contradicts the paper’s own earlier and later definitions, where MVP_pair refers to a dual-encoder architecture with parameter sharing, while MVP_obj uses a single encoder with an auxiliary MLM objective. The error occurs in a key definitional paragraph and risks significant confusion, as readers may conflate architecturally distinct methods. Though the correct architecture is recoverable from figures and later sections, the misstatement undermines clarity, methodological precision, and reproducibility. Multiple reported issues vary only in phrasing or focus (notation vs. logic), but describe the same root error. This consolidated version prioritizes clarity and severity.

comments.title: Missing and inconsistent loss function formulations for MVP_hier and MVP_obj
comments.quote:
The loss function for MVP _hier_ is

in which I _[x] i_[is a variable with binary values indicating whether the] _[ i]_[-th token is masked in] **[ x]**[.]
comments.explanation: The paper repeatedly claims to define loss functions for MVP_hier, MVP_obj, and MVP_pair but fails to provide complete mathematical expressions in the text. For MVP_hier, the loss is mentioned multiple times with only a masking indicator defined and no actual formula. The same occurs for MVP_obj: the loss is described verbally, with indicators I[x]_i and I[y]_i introduced, but no summation or objective function is displayed. Later, an equation for MVP_hier is presented, but it raises further issues: it suggests token-level masking, contradicting the stated use of whole-word masking. The absence of precise loss formulations—especially for methods whose novelty lies in training objectives—severely impacts reproducibility. While surrounding context and figures imply intent, the lack of formal expressions falls short of standard ML paper expectations and creates ambiguity in implementation. This combines issues related to missing equations, inconsistently defined masking, and underspecification across all three strategies.

comments.title: Underspecified and ambiguous vocabulary construction for _seg_
comments.quote: Finally, _seg_ with size _N_ is built with the following procedures: a) do CWS on the corpus; b) count the words’ frequency, and add the tokens from _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ with frequency 0; c) for long tail Chinese words and non-Chinese tokens, tokenize them into subword tokens with _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , and the words’ frequencies are added to the sub-word tokens’ frequencies; d) sort the vocab via frequency, and if the most frequent N words or tokens can cover 99.95% of the corpus, then take them as vocab.
comments.explanation: The construction of the _seg_ vocabulary lacks critical detail. Key phrases such as 'long tail Chinese words' and 'non-Chinese tokens' are undefined. The step to 'add tokens from _seg_tok with frequency 0' is conceptually unclear—why include zero-frequency tokens? It appears the intent is to incorporate subword segmentation rules, but this is poorly explained. Additionally, step (d) references 'words or tokens' without clarifying whether this includes merged subword units, and how duplicates or overlapping subwords are handled. The note that 'some of the tokens from _seg_tok will be dropped' contradicts the earlier inclusion of all such tokens, creating further confusion. These ambiguities prevent exact reproduction of the _seg_ vocabulary, which is used in comparisons, thus impacting the validity of experimental claims. The description is algorithmically underspecified despite being central to reproducibility.

comments.title: Ambiguous and underspecified masking strategy for dual-vocabulary training
comments.quote: For MVP _obj_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) and MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ), 1/3 of the time only tokens _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ are predicted, and 1/3 of the time only tokens from the derived char based vocab are predicted, and for the rest of the time, tokens from both vocabs are predicted.
comments.explanation: The paper introduces a dynamic masking schedule where only one vocabulary’s tokens are predicted in certain training steps, but fails to clarify how masking is applied. It states 'whole word masking is applied' unconditionally, yet the prediction regime varies. This raises a logical conflict: is masking fixed per input, or does it change based on which vocabulary is targeted? The absence of details on how masking probabilities are distributed—e.g., whether the 15% rate is split across regimes or applied per regime—leads to ambiguity in implementation. Additionally, it's unclear whether masking is aligned across tokenizations (e.g., masking a word affects both char and seg_tok representations). The current description risks misinterpretation as conditional input masking rather than task-specific loss computation. Given that this training strategy is novel and central to model performance, its underspecification undermines reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent and unclear notation for MVP_pair finetuning parameters
comments.quote: MVP _pair_ with two vocab _V_ 1 and _V_ 2 is denoted as MVP _pair_ ( _V_ 1 _, V_ 2 _, i, j_ ), where _i_ can be _single_ , meaning only keep the encoder from _V_ 1 for finetuning, or _ensemble_ , meaning keep both encoders, and _j_ can be _ftc_ or _ctf_, which is two approaches for finetuning MVP _pair_ on sequence labeling tasks.
comments.explanation: The notation for MVP_pair evolves across the paper: initially defined as MVP_pair(V1, V2, i), it is later expanded to include a fourth parameter j. This creates inconsistency, especially because j is only relevant when i=ensemble and on specific tasks like sequence labeling. Including j in the general notation overloads it and suggests broader applicability than evaluated. Furthermore, experiments only report results for i=single (MVP_pair(V1,V2,s)), with no mention of j, making it unclear whether ensemble configurations were tested. This notation overloading risks misleading readers into assuming a more flexible or general system than actually implemented. The forward reference without clarification harms clarity and precision.

comments.title: Ambiguous aggregation mechanism in MVP_hier compromises reproducibility
comments.quote: In MVP _hier_ , the embeddings of Chinese characters are aggregated to form the vector representations of multi-gram words or tokens, which then are fed into transformer encoders, and then the word based vocab will be used in masked language model (MLM) training.
comments.explanation: While the paper mentions that character-level representations are aggregated to form word-level inputs in MVP_hier, it never specifies the aggregation function (e.g., mean pooling, max pooling, first-token selection). In contrast, for MVP_obj, the aggregator is explicitly defined as a 'BERT-style pooler' using the starting subtoken. This asymmetry leaves MVP_hier underspecified. Given that the choice of aggregator can significantly impact model behavior and performance, omitting this detail hampers replication. Although common defaults might be assumed, the lack of explicit definition lowers methodological transparency, especially when the authors otherwise emphasize architectural distinctions.

comments.title: Overstatement of MVP_obj superiority in abstract without full empirical support
comments.quote: MVP _obj_ help to provide a better single vocab model than MVP _pair_.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims MVP_obj yields a 'better' single-vocab model than MVP_pair, but this is not fully supported by results. While MVP_obj performs strongly and is more efficient, MVP_pair(ensemble) achieves the highest scores in key tasks (e.g., 87.96 on chn), surpassing all MVP_obj variants. The term 'better' implies overall superiority, but the claim is primarily justified on efficiency grounds, not performance. MVP_obj is a strong, efficient alternative, but calling it superior without qualification misrepresents the comparison. The claim overreaches given that ensemble MVP_pair achieves better performance. This overstatement risks misleading readers, especially those relying on the abstract for a quick summary.

comments.title: Confusion between derived and baseline 'char' vocabulary across MVP variants
comments.quote: For MVP _hier_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ), MVP _obj_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) and MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ), the char based vocab in ... is derived from _seg tok_ by splitting Chinese characters of _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ tokens into characters, not exactly the same with _char_.
comments.explanation: The paper uses the label 'char' for two distinct vocabularies: the original baseline (from raw character segmentation) and a derived version (from splitting _seg_tok_ tokens). This conflation occurs in model notation (e.g., MVP_obj(char, seg_tok)), creating potential confusion about which 'char' is used where. The derived version may lack word boundaries and differ in distribution. By using the same symbol without distinction, the paper violates best practices in notation and risks misinterpretation when comparing models. While experts may resolve the difference, it increases cognitive load and threatens accurate comparison, especially for reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent handling of lambda (λ) weighting across MVP variants
comments.quote: For MVP _pair_ , ... MLM loss from both sides will be added with weights. ... Here λ is the coefficient which measures the relative importance of the auxiliary MLM task.
comments.explanation: The paper uses λ to weight the coarse-grained loss in both MVP_obj and MVP_pair, but fails to clarify the conceptual difference. In MVP_obj, the coarse loss is auxiliary and scaled by λ, while in MVP_pair, both losses are primary and symmetric unless weighted. The main text introduces λ in the context of MVP_obj as balancing an auxiliary task but reuses it for MVP_pair without redefining its role. This risks misinterpretation—readers might assume symmetry or default equal weighting. The assumption section states that λ applies to the coarse loss in MVP_pair, but this is not clear in the body. Without explicit distinctions, the notation blurs architectural differences and could lead to incorrect implementation.
