comments.title: Notation inconsistency for MVP_obj
comments.quote: We will denote this strategy as MVP_pair ( _V_ 1 _, V_ 2), where _V_ 1 and _V_ 2 are a pair of vocabs and _V_ 1 is the more fine-grained one.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce the third pre-training strategy as MVP_obj (depicted in Figure 2(b)) immediately prior to this sentence. However, the subsequent notation definition incorrectly labels it as MVP_pair(V_1, V_2). This directly contradicts the earlier definition of MVP_pair and will confuse readers when trying to map the formal notation to the described architecture. It should clearly be denoted as MVP_obj(V_1, V_2). This is a moderate issue as it affects notation consistency and makes tracking definitions across the paper unnecessarily difficult, though it does not undermine the core methodology.

comments.title: Citation mismatch for Google Chinese BERT
comments.quote: the vocabulary for these Chinese PLMs remain to be the one provided by Google Chinese Bert Devlin et al. (2018), which is based on Chinese characters.
comments.explanation: The reference 'Devlin et al. (2018)' corresponds to the original BERT paper ('BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding'), not 'Google Chinese BERT'. Google Chinese BERT is a distinct model adaptation released by Google China that modifies the original BERT for Chinese using a character-based vocabulary. Citing the original BERT paper as if it were the direct source of the Google Chinese BERT vocabulary is factually incorrect. This misattribution could mislead readers regarding the exact baseline vocabulary being used and compared against in the experiments.

comments.title: Inconsistent Notation for MVP_pair Arguments Across Sections
comments.quote: MVP_pair with two vocab V1 and V2 is denoted as MVP_pair(V1, V2, i, j), where i can be single, meaning only keep the encoder from V1 for finetuning, or ensemble, meaning keep both encoders, and j can be ftc ... or ctf
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by the change in the number and format of arguments for the MVP_pair notation between the introduction/abstract and the methods section. In the abstract and introduction, the authors define MVP_pair(V1, V2, i) with i∈{s, e}. However, in Section 3.2, they expand it to MVP_pair(V1, V2, i, j) with i∈{single, ensemble} and introduce a new j parameter. I checked the surrounding text to see if this was intentionally clarified later; the authors do note that j is only relevant for sequence labeling ensembles and that i=‘single’ negates j. While the underlying intent is clear and logically sound, the inconsistent notation across sections creates unnecessary friction for readers and could hinder reproducibility. The authors should unify the notation (e.g., consistently use MVP_pair(V1, V2, i, j) throughout, or explicitly map the abstract's shorthand to the full definition).

comments.title: Contradictory Figure Reference in MVP_hier Description
comments.quote: Figure 2(a) depicts the architecture of MVP_hier and Figure 2(a) depicts its procedure for processing input sentences.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify that MVP_hier combines fine-grained and coarse-grained vocabularies in a hierarchical structure, which is a sound architectural choice. However, I noticed the sentence references Figure 2(a) twice in the same breath. Given the paper's structure and the fact that Figure 1 illustrates tokenization workflows while Figure 2 shows MVP architectures, it is highly likely the second reference is a typographical error intending to point to Figure 1 or Figure 2(b). As written, a reader consulting Figure 2(a) for the processing procedure would find a mismatch. This should be corrected to avoid misleading the reader to the wrong visual aid.

comments.title: Ambiguous Phrasing of MVP_obj Representation Aggregation
comments.quote: For example, encoded representations of the chars ” ~~`喜`~~ ” and ” `欢` ” inside the word ” ~~`喜`~~ `欢` ” is aggregated to the vector representation of the word, and an auxiliary MLM layer is tasked to predict the word based on Vc .
comments.explanation: The authors rightly propose using an auxiliary coarse-grained MLM objective to enrich fine-grained representations. When describing how the word vector is formed, the main text states that 'encoded representations of the chars... is aggregated to the vector representation of the word,' which syntactically and semantically suggests pooling or combining multiple character representations. However, the immediately preceding sentence and footnote [4] clarify that the method specifically adopts a 'BERT-style pooler, which is to use the starting token’s representation to represent the word’s representation.' While the footnote resolves the technical ambiguity, the primary description is imprecise and could mislead readers into assuming a full-character aggregation mechanism is used. Clarifying the main text to explicitly state that only the initial character's representation is forwarded would align the prose with the implemented method and standard BERT conventions.

comments.title: Overclaim of consistent performance improvement contradicts table data
comments.quote: MVPs consistently improve the models’ performances, among which the largest improvements come from MVP_obj and ensemble MVP_pair.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a strong empirical generalization. However, cross-referencing with Table 1 reveals that `MVP_hier(seg_t_ok)` actually scores lower than the `char` baseline on two NER tasks: MSRA (80.57 vs 81.14) and CCKS (84.88 vs 85.63). Similarly, `MVP_obj(seg_t_ok, seg)` scores 81.05 on MSRA, which is also below the `char` baseline (81.14). While MVPs do show clear gains on sentence classification tasks, the claim of 'consistent' improvement across all settings is empirically inaccurate. This overgeneralization misleads readers about the robustness of the proposed strategies on span/sequence labeling tasks.

comments.title: Self-referential typo obscures efficiency comparison
comments.quote: In addition, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ is more efficient than _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ . we can observe a 1.35x speed up when changing the vocab from _char_ to _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ .
comments.explanation: The sentence literally states that `seg_t_ok` is more efficient than itself, which is a tautology and breaks the logical flow of the paragraph. The immediately following clause ('changing the vocab from char to seg_t_ok') makes it obvious the author intended to compare `seg_t_ok` against `char`. While context eventually resolves the confusion, the literal reading is nonsensical and could momentarily mislead reviewers or readers about the actual efficiency benchmark being reported. It requires a simple editorial fix.

comments.title: Ambiguous aggregator design for auxiliary MLM objective
comments.quote: For the aggregator in the example, we adopt BERT-style pooler, which is to use the starting token’s representation to represent the word’s representation.
comments.explanation: The authors describe using the hidden state of the first subword token to represent an entire multi-token word. In standard BERT architectures, the [CLS] token learns sentence-level semantics via cross-attention, not by simply being the initial token. Relying solely on the start token's raw representation ignores the internal semantic contribution of the remaining subwords within the word boundary. While Footnote 4 acknowledges this is a resource-motivated simplification, the current description leaves ambiguous how fine-grained contextual signals are aggregated for the coarse-grained prediction. Without clarifying whether other positions are ignored or averaged, reproducing the exact architectural behavior may be difficult.

comments.title: Underspecified fine-tuning batch size selection procedure
comments.quote: For finetuning, the sequence length is 256, the learning rate is 2e-5, the optimizer is Adam Kingma & Ba (2015) and the batch size is set as the power of 2 and so that each epoch contains less than 1000 steps.
comments.explanation: The method for determining the fine-tuning batch size is underspecified. The text states a constraint (power of 2, <1000 steps per epoch) but does not explain how this constraint is algorithmically satisfied across datasets of varying sizes. For example, is the step limit applied to the full validation set, or a subset? Is the batch size dynamically adjusted per dataset, or is there a default cap? Since batch size significantly impacts gradient noise and convergence behavior during fine-tuning of PLMs, omitting the calculation logic or implementation strategy hinders reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for MVP_pair configuration arguments
comments.quote: We will denote this strategy as MVP _pair_ ( _V_ 1 _, V_ 2 _, i_ ), where _V_ 1 and _V_ 2 are two different vocabs, _i_ = _s_ means only the encoder with _V_ 1 is kept for finetuning (single vocab model), and _i_ = _e_ means both encoders are kept (ensemble model).
comments.explanation: The introduction defines the second parameter `i` as shorthand (`s`/`e`), but Section 3.2 redefines it using full words (`single`/`ensemble`) and adds a fourth parameter `j` (ftc/ctf). Subsequently, the paper uses mixed notation in the results section and Table 1 (e.g., `MVP_pair(char, seg_t_ok, s)` alongside `MVP_pair(char, seg_t_ok, e, ftc)`). While a careful reader can mentally map these variants, the shifting symbol definitions and inconsistent use of abbreviations vs. full strings across sections create unnecessary cognitive load and risk misinterpretation of the exact model configurations being reported.

comments.title: Notation typo in MVP_obj definition
comments.quote: We will denote this strategy as MVP _pair_ ( _V_ 1 _, V_ 2), where _V_ 1 and _V_ 2 are a pair of vocabs and _V_ 1 is the more fine-grained one.
comments.explanation: In the paragraph introducing the third MVP variant, the authors explicitly state it is denoted as MVP_obj earlier in the same paragraph. However, the notation definition mistakenly copies the naming convention from the previous paragraph (MVP_pair) and writes 'MVP _pair_ ( _V_ 1 _, V_ 2)' instead of 'MVP _obj_ ( _V_ 1 _, V_ 2)'. This is a clear copy-paste error that creates a notation inconsistency, as the text later refers to MVP_obj using the correct notation but the initial definition is incorrect.

comments.title: Underspecified weighting mechanism for MVP_pair loss
comments.quote:
MLM loss from both sides will be added with weights. ... Thus the loss function for MVP _pair_ is
If after MVP _pair_ pretraining we decide to only keep one of the encoder, we will call this model single vocab MVP _pair_ .
comments.explanation: The authors state that the two MLM losses are 'added with weights' and later report hyperparameter tuning for $\lambda$ across values like 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, etc. However, unlike MVP_obj where $\lambda$ is explicitly tied to the auxiliary task loss, the paper never specifies how $\lambda$ is applied to the MVP_pair objective (e.g., $L = L_{V_1} + \lambda L_{V_2}$ or normalized). Without the explicit loss equation or a clear definition of how the weights relate to $\lambda$, readers cannot accurately reproduce the pretraining procedure. The equation itself is also omitted due to OCR, compounding the issue.

comments.title: Redundant sequence definition in MVP_hier section
comments.quote: Denote **x** and **y** denote the sequences of tokens with length _lx_ and _ly_ , for the same sentence under _Vc_ and _Vf_ , in which a part of tokens are masked. ... The loss function for MVP _hier_ is ... in which I _[x] i_[is a variable with binary values indicating whether the] _[ i]_[-th token is masked in] **[ x]**[.]
comments.explanation: The text introduces sequences for both the coarse-grained vocab ($V_c$, denoted **x**) and the fine-grained vocab ($V_f$, denoted **y**) in the MVP_hier subsection. However, the subsequent loss function description and masking strategy exclusively reference $V_c$ and **x**. The introduction of **y** and $V_f$ here is redundant for MVP_hier (which only predicts on $V_c$ tokens) and may confuse readers about whether fine-grained tokens participate in the hierarchical MLM task. It is likely borrowed from the MVP_obj description that follows.

comments.title: Imprecise terminology for word-level aggregation
comments.quote: For the aggregator in the example, we adopt BERT-style pooler, which is to use the starting token’s representation to represent the word’s representation.
comments.explanation: The authors refer to using the starting token's raw representation as a 'BERT-style pooler'. Standard BERT pooling involves passing the [CLS] token through a learned linear layer followed by a tanh activation. Using the start token directly is a simplification that reduces parameters but deviates from the standard definition. The authors do acknowledge this in Footnote 4, noting that alternative aggregators are left for future work. While not a fatal flaw, calling it a 'BERT-style pooler' without qualification could mislead practitioners expecting the standard architectural component.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a practical yet understudied challenge in Chinese natural language processing by proposing a redesigned vocabulary ("seg_tok") and three Multi-Vocab Pretraining (MVP) strategies to balance the efficiency of character-based models with the semantic expressiveness of word-based models. The experimental design is systematic and demonstrates clear improvements in downstream performance, particularly for sequence labeling tasks. However, the overall clarity of the contributions is weakened by repetitive phrasing in the definitions of the three MVP variants (MVP_hier, MVP_pair, and MVP_obj), where the textual descriptions of the auxiliary objectives and forward passes appear conflated or overly dense, making it difficult to immediately distinguish the architectural differences depicted in Figure 2. A more concise and structurally distinct presentation of these strategies is recommended to better articulate their novelty.

comments.title: Incorrect Notation Label in Definition
comments.quote: The third version of MVP (denoted as MVP_obj)... We will denote this strategy as MVP_pair (V1, V2), where V1 and V2 are a pair of vocabs and V1 is the more fine-grained one.
comments.explanation: In the introduction section, the authors define the third version of the MVP framework as "MVP_obj" (Multi-Vocab Pretraining with Objectives) and describe its architecture in detail. However, in the defining sentence, they mistakenly label it as "MVP_pair" (Multi-Vocab Pretraining with Paired Encoders). This is a direct notation inconsistency that could confuse readers regarding the distinction between the objectives-based strategy and the paired-encoder strategy.

comments.title: Missing or Corrupted Mathematical Definitions
comments.quote:
The loss function for MVP_hier is

in which I[x] i_[is a variable with binary values indicating whether the] [ i] [-th token is masked in] **[ x]**[.]
comments.explanation: The provided passage claims to define the loss functions for MVP_hier, MVP_obj, and MVP_pair. For MVP_hier and MVP_obj, the actual mathematical expression (e.g., the sum of the masked language model losses) is completely missing or rendered as garbled LaTeX text by OCR (e.g., `I[x] i_` instead of `I[x]_i`). For MVP_pair, the sentence simply ends with "Thus the loss function for MVP_pair is" without providing any formula. While the text describes the components of the loss (masking indicators, lambda weights), the fundamental equations are not present in the provided text, making it impossible to verify the formulation.

comments.title: Numerical Statistic Applies to Wrong Vocab Type
comments.quote: Vocab size is 21128 for char and seg_tok. 50.38% of seg are Chinese tokens with length more than 1.
comments.explanation: In Section 4.1, the authors distinguish between three vocab types: `char` (sub-word on chars), `seg_tok` (sub-word on CWS results), and `seg` (high-frequency word vocab). The statement assigns a specific statistic ("50.38% of seg are Chinese tokens with length more than 1") to the `seg` vocabulary, which is defined as a list of high-frequency *words*. In a Chinese word segmentation context, high-frequency word vocabularies naturally consist of multi-character tokens, so asserting that 50.38% of them have length > 1 is contradictory or misleading. The number "50.38%" strongly suggests a statistic related to the subword tokenizer (seg_tok) with a vocab size of 21,128, where many tokens are split multi-character words. This appears to be a typo where `seg` was written instead of `seg_tok`.

comments.title: Formula Index Notation Errors (OCR Artifacts)
comments.quote: in which I[x] i_[is a variable with binary values indicating whether the] [ i] [-th token is masked in] **[ x]**[.]
comments.explanation: In the text describing the loss function indicators for MVP_hier and MVP_obj, the mathematical notation for the indices is corrupted by OCR errors (e.g., `I[x] i_` instead of `I[x]_i`). While the meaning is recoverable from context (masking indicator variables), the presence of these errors in the equation description makes the formal definition difficult to read and suggests errors in the underlying typesetting or extraction process.

comments.title: Missing mathematical definition for MVP_pair loss
comments.quote: Formally, Thus the loss function for MVP pair is
comments.explanation: The authors explicitly introduce the MVP_pair strategy and signal the presentation of a formal definition with the phrase "Formally, Thus the loss function for MVP pair is". However, the mathematical formula is never provided in the text, and the text proceeds immediately to describe the single vocab vs. ensemble configurations. This omission prevents readers from verifying the loss formulation, understanding the trade-offs between $V_1$ and $V_2$ in the objective function, or reproducing the results.

comments.title: Symbol 'i' overloaded between masking indices and configuration parameters
comments.quote: MVP_pair (V_ 1 _, V_ 2 _, i, j_) , where _i_ can be _single_ , meaning only keep the encoder from _V_ 1 for finetuning...
comments.explanation: In the immediately preceding paragraph (describing MVP_hier), the symbol $i$ is used as an index inside the mask indicator variable $I_{x_i}$ (indicating the $i$-th token's masking status). In the MVP_pair notation section, $i$ is redefined as a discrete configuration parameter (where it can be 'single' or 'ensemble'). While this is resolved by the explicit context in the current sentence, it is a notation inconsistency that could confuse readers following the transition from the loss function definitions to the notation summary.

comments.title: Ambiguous sentence structure regarding vocabulary statistics
comments.quote: The vocab size is 21128 for _char_ and _seg_ ~~t~~ok . 50.38% of _seg_ are Chinese tokens with length more than 1.
comments.explanation: The sentence immediately follows defining the vocabulary size for `_seg tok_`. It then makes a claim starting with "50.38% of _seg_..." where `_seg_` refers to a distinct, large hybrid vocabulary defined later in the paragraph. While contextually the statistic describes `_seg_` (which retains natural words), the punctuation (a period before the statistic) could be misinterpreted by a reader as referring to `_seg tok_` (the subject of the previous clause). This linguistic ambiguity, while fixable, creates a potential stumbling block.

comments.title: Missing Primary Citation
comments.quote: The passage includes citations to 'Devlin et al. (2018)' in the Abstract and Introduction (e.g., 'vocabulary provided by Google Chinese Bert Devlin et al. (2018)'), but this specific reference is not present in the provided reference list.
comments.explanation: The paper's core motivation is to critique and improve upon the vocabulary of Google Chinese BERT (Devlin et al., 2018). While the context mentions citations like Radford et al. (2018) for GPT, Peters et al. (2018) for ELMo, and mentions BERT multiple times, the foundational citation to BERT is absent from the bibliography. Even assuming the OCR missed the first entry or that it refers to the seminal BERT paper (which is not listed), this omission undermines the review of the literature and the paper's own claims about the status quo of Chinese PLMs. It is a fundamental documentation flaw for an experimental paper.

In a Chinese word segmentation context, high-frequency word vocabularies naturally consist of multi-character tokens, so asserting that 50.38% of them have length > 1 is contradictory or misleading. The number "50.38%" strongly suggests a statistic related to the subword tokenizer (seg_tok) with a vocab size of 21,128, where many tokens are split multi-character words. This appears to be a typo where `seg` was written instead of `seg_tok`.

However, the mathematical formula is never provided in the text, and the text proceeds immediately to describe the single vocab vs. ensemble configurations. This omission prevents readers from verifying the loss formulation, understanding the trade-offs between $V_1$ and $V_2$ in the objective function, or reproducing the results.

comments.explanation: In the immediately preceding paragraph (describing MVP_hier), the symbol $i$ is used as an index inside the mask indicator variable $I_{x_i}$ (indicating the $i$-th token's masking status). In the MVP_pair notation section, $i$ is redefined as a discrete configuration parameter (where it can be 'single' or 'ensemble'). While this is resolved by the explicit context in the current sentence, it is a notation inconsistency that could confuse readers following the transition from the loss function definitions to the notation summary.

comments.title: Ambiguous sentence structure regarding vocabulary statistics
comments.quote: The vocab size is 21128 for _char_ and _seg_ ~~t~~ok . 50.38% of _seg_ are Chinese tokens with length more than 1.
comments.explanation: The sentence immediately follows defining the vocabulary size for `_seg tok_`. It then makes a claim starting with "50.38% of _seg_..." where `_seg_` refers to a distinct, large hybrid vocabulary defined later in the paragraph. While contextually the statistic describes `_seg_` (which retains natural words), the punctuation (a period before the statistic) could be misinterpreted by a reader as referring to `_seg tok_` (the subject of the previous clause). This linguistic ambiguity, while fixable, creates a potential stumbling block.

comments.title: Missing Primary Citation
comments.quote: The passage includes citations to 'Devlin et al. (2018)' in the Abstract and Introduction (e.g., 'vocabulary provided by Google Chinese Bert Devlin et al. (2018)'), but this specific reference is not present in the provided reference list.
comments.explanation: The paper's core motivation is to critique and improve upon the vocabulary of Google Chinese BERT (Devlin et al., 2018). While the context mentions citations like Radford et al. (2018) for GPT, Peters et al. (2018) for ELMo, and mentions BERT multiple times, the foundational citation to BERT is absent from the bibliography. Even assuming the OCR missed the first entry or that it refers to the seminal BERT paper (which is not listed), this omission undermines the review of the literature and the paper's own claims about the status quo of Chinese PLMs. It is a fundamental documentation flaw for an experimental paper.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an interesting and practical problem—optimizing Chinese BERT vocabularies through word segmentation and multi-vocabulary pretraining—but suffers from several significant issues that undermine its quality and clarity. The writing is often unclear and contains numerous typos (e.g., “seg ~~t~~ ok” with strikethrough, missing references like “Albert ?”), inconsistent notation (MVP_pair vs. MVP_obj naming confusion), and incomplete figure captions. The motivation for multi-vocab pretraining is not well justified beyond vague references to prior work, and the proposed MVP variants are described in a confusing, overlapping manner. The experimental setup is insufficiently detailed (only Albert base, one Wikipedia corpus, no baseline comparisons for different vocabulary sizes or CWS toolkits), and the results are reported only in summary without quantitative evidence in the provided text. Additionally, the paper lacks a clear discussion of how the different MVP strategies relate to each other and why they would be expected to improve expressiveness. Overall, while the core idea has merit, the paper requires substantial revision to improve readability, clarify methodological contributions, and provide rigorous empirical validation.

comments.title: Inconsistent naming of MVP_obj as MVP_pair
comments.quote: The third version of MVP (denoted as MVP_obj) is depicted in Figure 2(b). ... We will denote this strategy as MVP_pair (V1, V2), where V1 and V2 are a pair of vocabs and V1 is the more fine-grained one.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce MVP_obj as the third version of multi-vocab pretraining, but then immediately say they will denote it as MVP_pair. This is almost certainly a copy-paste or labeling error; the correct notation should be MVP_obj(V1, V2). Since MVP_pair is already defined earlier for a different architecture (Figure 2(c)), reusing the same name for this strategy creates ambiguity and could lead a reader to confuse two fundamentally different methods (one with separate encoders, one with a single encoder and auxiliary objective). The context does not resolve this because the paragraph clearly describes MVP_obj's mechanism (single encoder, fine-grained vocab, auxiliary coarse-grained prediction) which is distinct from MVP_pair's twin-encoder setup.

comments.title: Contradictory assignment of left/right encoder vocab in MVP_pair examples
comments.quote: For example, the word '篮球' (basketball) is masked. The left encoder, which is with seg_tok, has to predict the single masked token is '篮球', and the right encoder has to predict '篮' and '球' for two masked tokens.
comments.explanation: In the first example, left = seg_tok (predicts 1 token), right = char (predicts 2 tokens). Later, in the description of MVP_pair after the picture text, the authors write: 'For example in Figure 2(c), '喜欢' is masked, and thus two tokens on the left sequence and(or) one token on the right sequence are masked. The left encoder and MLM layer is tasked to recover the two tokens '喜' and '欢'. And on the right, the other MLM layer needs to recover '喜欢'.' Here, left predicts two tokens (implying char) and right predicts one token (implying seg_tok). This directly contradicts the first example. Since the paper only considers the pair between seg_tok and char, the assignment of which encoder uses which vocab should be consistent. The contradiction confuses the architecture: a reader cannot tell whether the left encoder is supposed to operate on seg_tok or char tokens. Additionally, the later text uses the notation MVP_pair(char, seg_tok) but does not specify whether that ordering corresponds to left/right or to V1/V2, further compounding the ambiguity.

comments.title: Missing citation for ALBERT model
comments.quote: and Albert ? (base model) as our PLM.
comments.explanation: The text includes a question mark where a citation for ALBERT should appear. Given the context and the reference to 'Albert' (presumably ALBERT Lan et al., 2019), the placeholder '?' is likely an incomplete LaTeX citation that was not resolved. While this does not affect the scientific content, it is an oversight that should be corrected for reproducibility and proper attribution.

comments.title: Incomplete loss function equations for MVP_hier and MVP_obj due to OCR truncation
comments.quote: Let **x** and **y** denote the sequences of tokens with length _lx_ and _ly_ , for the same sentence under _Vc_ and _Vf_ , in which a part of tokens are masked. Denote **x** _[mask]_ as the masked tokens under _Vc_ . The loss function for MVP _hier_ is in which I _[x] i_[is a variable with binary values indicating whether the] _[ i]_[-th token is masked in] **[ x][.]]**  ... The loss function for MVP _obj_ is as follows: ... in which I _[x] i_[and][I] _[y] i_[are][variables][with binary][values indicating whether][the] _[i]_[-th token][is masked][in] sequence x and y respectively. Here _λ_ is the coefficient which measures the relative importance of the auxiliary MLM task.
comments.explanation: The provided text contains truncated equations and fragmented descriptions for the loss functions of MVP_hier (paragraph 22) and MVP_obj (paragraphs 24–25), likely due to OCR artifacts. For MVP_hier, only the beginning of the loss function is shown, and the full formula is missing. For MVP_obj, the equation itself is missing entirely, though the surrounding explanation of indicator variables and the weighting coefficient is partially recoverable. These issues do not affect the intended meaning but should be corrected for completeness.

comments.title: Underspecified ensemble finetuning for sequence labeling
comments.quote: For sequence labeling tasks, two ways of ensemble can be conducted... we will call this approach fine-to-coarse ensemble. The other approach is coarse-to-fine ensemble... Then the labels of " ~~`喜`~~ " and " `欢` " are predicted.
comments.explanation: The ensemble methods for sequence labeling are described at a high level—concatenating representations from one encoder to the other—but without specifying how the label predictions are combined or whether any aggregation (e.g., averaging, attention) is used before the final classification layer. For the 'fine-to-coarse' approach, it says 'representations ... are aggregated to the representation ... and it will be concatenated to the representation from the coarse-grained encoder', suggesting a concatenation followed by a standard classifier, which is plausible. However, the 'coarse-to-fine' method says 'concatenate the representation ... to ... from the fine-grained encoder', which might imply per-token concatenation, but the exact mechanism for distributing the coarse representation to multiple fine-grained tokens is not specified. This ambiguity could make reproduction difficult for practitioners. However, given that the paper is under review and these are introduced as high-level strategies, and standard practices (e.g., concatenating features before linear classifier) are common in the field, this may be acceptable for a conference paper that provides figures and code.

comments.title: Inconsistent indicator notation for masked tokens: passage uses I for both sequences, but earlier definition uses J for the second sequence
comments.quote: in which I _[x] i_[and][I] _[y] i_[are][variables][with binary][values indicating whether][the] _[i]_[-th token][is masked][in] sequence x and y respectively.
comments.explanation: The passage describes the indicator variables for the MVP_obj loss as I[x_i] and I[y_i] for sequences x and y. However, in the summary's earlier presentation of the MVP_obj loss (L = -∑_{i: I[x_i]=1} log P(x_i | x, y) - ∑_{j: J[y_j]=1} log P(y_j | x, y)), a distinct symbol J is used for the indicator on y. While the passage's use of I for both is simpler and likely the intended notation (J may be an OCR artifact), a careful reader might notice the mismatch if they refer back to the formal equation. This could cause momentary confusion about whether a different indicator function is being introduced. The context does not explicitly resolve whether the original paper uses I for both or distinguishes them, so the inconsistency is worth flagging for clarity.

comments.title: Overclaim that 'MVPs consistently improve the models’ performances'
comments.quote: First, we can see MVPs consistently improve the models’ performances, among which the largest improvements come from MVP_obj and ensemble MVP_pair.
comments.explanation: The table shows that MVP_hier(seg_tok) performs worse than the single-vocab seg_tok on all five sentence classification tasks (e.g., chn: 87.06 vs. 87.17; br: 78.67 vs. 79.08; lcqmc: 79.64 vs. 79.79; xnli: 59.89 vs. 60.19; nlpcc: 64.06 vs. 64.02). While MVP_hier does improve on NER tasks, the statement 'consistently improve' over all models and tasks is not supported. The authors later acknowledge that the largest improvements come from the other MVP variants, but the unqualified phrase 'consistently improve' could mislead a reader into thinking every MVP variant outperforms its single-vocab counterpart on all tasks. This is a minor overclaim; the overall thrust of the paper is not undermined.
